entry.title: Contact Person determination based on Sales Area Partner Funtions in Service transactions
entry.content:
Background:

S/4 Private Cloud greenfield implementation of Service Management.

Statement:

Contact persons are maintained only in CP partner function under Customer > Sales Area - not as "real contact persons" with the BP relationship (because that is "general level" connection, not specific to sales area)! This is because there are different CPs in different Sales Areas.

To be achieved:

1.  When new service order/quotation/contract is created with certain SP in specific sales area, the CP determination should retrieve the correct contact based on the CP partner function assignment within sales area i.q. for that Sold-to.

2. Functional location created in specific Sales Area - the correct contact person should be assigned based on sales area partner functions (note: there is no BP relationship "is contact person for")

How can we achieve this?

entry.title: Exploring the RISE with SAP Methodology: A Smarter Path to ERP Transformation
entry.content:
Why RISE with SAP Matters

As businesses increasingly adopt AI-driven processes, ERP systems have become the trusted foundation for delivering reliable, business-ready intelligence. RISE with SAP offers a comprehensive transformation journey that combines proven methodologies, expert guidance, and AI-powered tools to simplify what has traditionally been a complex migration process.

The goal is clear: accelerate ERP modernisation while reducing risk, minimising complexity, and enabling organisations to realise AI-driven value faster.

Agent-Led Transformation: Modernising with Confidence

One of the defining features of RISE with SAP is its agent-led transformation approach, which leverages AI assistants to automate time-consuming activities and guide organisations through technical and business process changes.

By combining standardised delivery models with clean core principles, organisations can potentially reduce migration efforts by up to 35% while maintaining flexibility for future innovation.

This approach focuses on:

  • Simplifying migration and modernisation activities.
  • Automating repetitive and complex tasks.
  • Supporting business process transformation alongside technical upgrades.
  • Building a clean core environment that simplifies future updates and innovation.

A Proven Framework for Cloud Transformation

At the heart of the methodology is a standardised framework that guides every transformation project through six key phases:

1. Discover

Organisations define their business vision, assess their current landscape, and establish project objectives.

2. Prepare

Project teams complete onboarding activities, establish governance structures, and create a roadmap aligned with clean core principles.

3. Explore

Business capabilities, requirements, and AI opportunities are evaluated while defining the target solution.

4. Realise

Solutions are configured, adopted, and validated through change management and iterative implementation.

5. Deploy

Comprehensive testing is completed before moving the transformed ERP system into production.

6. Run

After go-live, organisations focus on operational excellence, continuous monitoring, and ongoing innovation.

Each phase incorporates quality gates to ensure alignment with project objectives and adherence to clean core best practices.

The Three Pillars of the RISE with SAP Methodology

The methodology combines three complementary elements to create a holistic transformation experience.

Standardized Framework

Best-practice roadmaps and structured project templates help organisations tailor their transformation while maintaining consistency and governance.

Expert Guidance

SAP experts and certified partners provide guidance throughout every stage of the journey, enhanced by AI-powered recommendations and proven transformation experience gained across thousands of implementations.

Agent-Led Toolchain

An integrated suite of tools supports business transformation across people, processes, applications, and data while accelerating migration and modernisation activities.

AI-Powered Tools Driving Transformation

The agent-led toolchain includes technologies that support every aspect of the journey:

  • Digital discovery assessment tools for evaluating business processes.
  • SAP Signavio solutions for process analysis and optimisation.
  • SAP LeanIX for enterprise architecture visibility and planning.
  • SAP Cloud ALM for roadmap execution, governance, and continuous improvement.
  • SAP Build, Integration Suite, and Business AI capabilities for enterprise innovation.
  • WalkMe solutions to improve user adoption and digital learning.
  • Partner solutions for data management, test automation, and document management.
  • SAP Joule serves as the intelligent engagement layer.

Together, these tools create an end-to-end transformation ecosystem rather than a collection of disconnected solutions.

Seven AI Assistants Simplifying Migration

RISE with SAP introduces seven specialised AI assistants that address some of the most challenging aspects of ERP modernisation:

  • System Analysis builds a trusted understanding of the existing environment.
  • Custom Code Assistant identifies and modernises custom developments while supporting clean core adoption.
  • Data Management Assistant detects and resolves data quality issues.
  • Configuration Assistant recommends optimised cloud ERP configurations.
  • Test Management Assistant automates testing activities and regression validation.
  • Rollout Assistant supports consistent multi-country and multi-region deployments.
  • Project Management Assistant provides continuous guidance, insights, and decision support throughout the transformation lifecycle.

These assistants reduce manual effort while making projects more predictable and data-driven.

Continuous Value Beyond Go-Live

The benefits of RISE with SAP extend well beyond the initial migration. Once systems are live, AI-powered capabilities continue to optimise ERP operations through:

  • Continuous custom code optimisation.
  • Automated testing and regression management.
  • Proactive monitoring of data quality.
  • Intelligent change and release management.

Instead of relying on large, disruptive upgrade projects, organisations can continuously improve their ERP landscape based on real operational insights.

Final Thoughts

The RISE with SAP Methodology represents more than a migration strategy—it is a comprehensive transformation framework designed for the AI era. By combining standardised project governance, expert guidance, and intelligent automation, it enables organisations to modernise ERP systems with greater speed, predictability, and confidence.

For businesses seeking to unlock the full potential of AI while maintaining a clean, future-ready ERP foundation, RISE with SAP offers a practical and structured path toward becoming an Autonomous Enterprise.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 19 - CDS source formatter
Upcoming Migration Webinars: Accelerate your transition from PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
If you're still running your integrations on SAP PI/PO, you already know what's coming. On-premise integration has had its day - and the window to act is now!

To help you move forward with confidence, we're hosting a two migration webinars dedicated to one thing: getting you from PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite without the guesswork.

Why now?

SAP PI/PO served its purpose. But the integration landscape has changed. Modern enterprise architecture demands cloud-native flexibility, real-time connectivity, and the ability to scale fast - across SAP and non-SAP systems alike. SAP Integration Suite delivers exactly that: a single, unified platform that consolidates your entire integration landscape and opens the door to AI-powered automation. Staying on PI/PO isn't standing still. It's falling behind.

What you'll learn:

SAP PI/PO Migration — The Big Picture
Where does your migration journey start, and where does it lead? We'll set the stage with a concise overview of the move from PI/PO and what success looks like.

Assessment Tool - See It in Action
Get introduced to the migration assessment capability and watch a live demo of its key features. Know where you stand before you make a single move.

Migration Tool - How It Actually Works
A practical walkthrough of the migration tooling - what it does, how it supports your transition, and what to expect when you use it.

What's New in the Community
Latest resources, documentation updates, and community contributions worth knowing about. Stay current without having to dig for it.

What's Coming Next
A snapshot of the migration roadmap - upcoming milestones, priorities, and planned enhancements. So you can plan ahead, not just react.

Q&A
Open floor. Bring your questions, edge cases, and real-world scenarios. We'll tackle them live.

 

How to join?

Interested? Great! Then make sure you register for one of following migration Webinars via the links below:

 

  • Session in English: 14th of July 2026  [Register here]
  • Session in Chinese: 16th of July 2026 [Register here]

 

See you there!

entry.title: Project Cost Line Items – Loss of Cumulative and Commitment-Based Measures in Recent Releases
entry.content:
Project Cost Line Items – Loss of Cumulative and Commitment-Based Measures in Recent Releases

I would like to raise a concern regarding the evolution of the Project Cost Line Items application.

In older S/4HANA releases, the application F2538 – Project Cost Line Items provided several analytical measures that were extremely useful for project controlling and cost analysis.

Reference:

  • SAP Help document 

  • SAP Fiori App Library: F2538

As shown in SAP documentation and screenshots from earlier releases, users could analyze project costs using measures such as:

  • Cumulative Actual Cost

  • Cumulative Planned Cost

  • Cumulative Actual Cost + Commitments

  • Commitment values originating from open Purchase Requisitions and Purchase Orders

These measures enabled the creation of trend analyses and S-curves, allowing project managers to compare:

  • Planned vs. Actual evolution over time

  • Actual + Commitment evolution over time

  • Cost performance by fiscal period and fiscal year using cumulative values

The chart below illustrates the type of cumulative analysis that was possible in previous releases, where values evolved period after period rather than being displayed as isolated figures.

 

Since S/4HANA 2023, the application has been replaced by F6992 – Project Costs – Line Items.

Reference:

  • SAP Help document 

  • SAP Fiori App Library: F6992

However, the newer application appears to have lost several important analytical capabilities.

The cumulative measures mentioned above are no longer available, and commitment-related measures seem to have been removed as well. As a result:

  • Users can only analyze values for individual periods.

  • Cumulative cost evolution cannot be displayed.

  • Actual Cost + Commitments analysis is no longer possible.

  • Commitment visibility (open Purchase Requisitions and Purchase Orders) is missing.

  • Building project cost S-curves directly from the standard application is no longer feasible.

From a project controlling perspective, this feels like a regression in reporting capabilities rather than an enhancement.

Has anyone found an alternative standard application that provides the same cumulative and commitment-based analysis?

Is there any roadmap item, SAP Note, or Customer Influence request addressing the reintroduction of these measures in F6992?

I would be interested in understanding how other customers and consultants are handling this limitation in S/4HANA 2023 and 2025.

Regards
Marcio Blos

entry.title: Re: Human-in-the-Loop SAP Agents: Approval, Escalation, and Audit (Series 2, Part 5 of 6)
entry.content:
@venkatamandavilli 

Thank you — and you've identified exactly the distinction that matters most in production. Human-in-the-loop as a checkbox produces systems that pause for approval on every action regardless of risk level, which defeats the purpose of automation entirely. Human-in-the-loop as a design pattern means you've thought through which decisions genuinely require human judgment, which can be handled autonomously at high confidence, and what the escalation path looks like when the agent isn't sure.

The SAP context makes this especially consequential because the actions agents take — raising a purchase order, posting a journal entry, routing a change request — have downstream effects that can be difficult or impossible to reverse. That irreversibility is what makes the audit trail non-negotiable rather than nice-to-have. When something goes wrong in a production SAP environment, the first question is always who authorized this and what was the reasoning. An agent without an audit trail can't answer that question.

Part 6 of the series covers how the audit trail integrates with the broader production observability layer — worth reading alongside this one if you're designing for enterprise deployment.

entry.title: How the Advanced Success Plan for SAP CX Operationalizes Hyper-Personalization at Scale
entry.content:
The Gap Between Personalization Strategy and Execution

Most organizations have accepted that personalization is no longer a differentiator — it is table stakes. Customers expect brands to know them, anticipate their needs, and engage them with relevance across every channel and touchpoint. The challenge is not agreeing on the vision. The challenge is closing the gap between that vision and systematic, scalable execution.

For many Commerce Cloud and Engagement Cloud customers, this gap shows up in familiar ways: recommendation engines that surface generic results because behavioural data is not connected, email campaigns timed by calendar rather than by individual habit, loyalty programs that reward transactions rather than relationships, and personalization rules that require significant manual effort to maintain at scale.

The ambition is there. The infrastructure to fulfil it, however, is often partial — clean data is siloed, AI capabilities are underutilised, and the organisational discipline to run ongoing experimentation does not yet exist.

This is precisely the problem the SAP Advanced Success Plan for Customer Experience is designed to solve.

 

What Hyper-Personalization Actually Requires

True hyper-personalization is not a feature you switch on. It is a capability you build systematically across three interdependent layers: data, decisioning, and delivery.

 

  1. Data is the foundation. Hyper-personalization requires unified, consent-aware, real-time customer profiles — consolidated across commerce transactions, engagement history, browsing behaviour, service interactions, and loyalty activity. Without this foundation, even the most sophisticated AI models are operating on incomplete signals.
  2. Decisioning is where AI translates those signals into action — the next best product to surface, the right offer to present, the optimal moment to reach out. This layer requires not just model accuracy but governance: knowing when to trust the algorithm and when human judgment should override it.
  3. Delivery is where the personalized experience reaches the customer — on the storefront, in the inbox, through a mobile push, across a loyalty interaction. This layer requires orchestration across channels, consistent with the customer's current context.

 

The Advanced Success Plan addresses all three layers simultaneously, providing the expert guidance, governance frameworks, and adoption acceleration needed to move from point capabilities to an integrated operating model.

 

Hyper-Personalization in SAP Commerce Cloud

SAP Commerce Cloud provides the storefront execution layer for personalization at scale. The platform's AI-assisted product recommendations capability enables organizations to show the most relevant products to each visitor at the right point in their shopping journey — from trending products and related items to complimentary products that support cross-sell and upsell motions. This goes beyond manual merchandising rules; it responds dynamically to real-time behavioural signals, improving conversion performance and driving product discovery at a scale no merchandising team could replicate manually.

Yet many Commerce Cloud customers have not yet activated the full depth of these capabilities. The blockers are predictable: data quality gaps that limit recommendation model performance, integration complexity between the commerce layer and upstream profile data, and an absence of the experimentation discipline needed to tune and improve models over time.

The Advanced Success Plan brings targeted guidance to address exactly these barriers. Data readiness assessments establish the quality baselines and integration patterns required to feed reliable signals into Commerce Cloud's personalization engine. Adoption accelerators help teams operationalize experimentation — defining hypotheses, running A/B tests, and translating results into durable configuration changes. The outcome is a storefront that continuously learns and improves, rather than one frozen at the point of initial configuration.

Hyper-Personalization in SAP Engagement Cloud

SAP Engagement Cloud (powered by SAP Emarsys) extends personalization beyond the storefront and into the full lifecycle of the customer relationship. This is where Commerce Cloud's transactional signals combine with engagement history to power cross-channel personalization that is genuinely individual rather than segment-based.

The platform's AI-assisted send time optimization is a direct example of this philosophy in practice. Rather than sending campaigns on a fixed schedule, the capability analyzes each contact's behavioural patterns — independently of time zone, language, or region — and delivers messages at the precise time each individual is most likely to engage. This is not personalization as a concept; it is personalization as an automated, scalable operational process.

Paired with AI-assisted campaign translation and omnichannel orchestration, Engagement Cloud enables marketing teams to move from building campaigns to orchestrating journeys — where the system is continuously learning which signals should trigger which interactions, and adapting those interactions based on what drives response.

The native integration between SAP Commerce Cloud and SAP Emarsys Customer Engagement is a critical accelerator here. By unifying commerce behaviour and engagement data, organizations can drive increases in conversion rate, purchase frequency, and average order value in ways that neither system could achieve independently. The Advanced Success Plan helps customers realize this joint value by aligning integration architecture, data governance, and adoption milestones across both products within a single, coordinated engagement model.

How the Advanced Success Plan Makes It Continuous Improvement

Hyper-personalization projects are often treated as one-time implementations. The Advanced Success Plan is designed to make them repeatable, continuously improving programs. This means:

  • Outcome-based governance: Co-defining the KPIs that matter — conversion rate lift, repeat purchase rate, engagement open rates, average order value — and building work streams aligned to move them measurably.
  • Prescriptive adoption patterns: Structured play-books for activating AI-assisted recommendations, send time optimization, and next-best action logic, with clear milestones and measurable gates.
  • Continuous enablement: Role-based coaching for the teams responsible for data, product ownership, and campaign operations — closing skills gaps that otherwise cause personalization programs to plateau or regress.
  • Proactive telemetry: Regular adoption checks that surface underperforming configurations before they impact business outcomes, and AI-guided best practices that inform ongoing tuning.

 

Making the Business Case Concrete

For Commerce Cloud customers, the value of operationalized hyper-personalization shows in storefront metrics: higher conversion from AI-surfaced recommendations, increased average order value through intelligent cross-sell, and improved product discovery that reduces bounce and exit rates.

For Engagement Cloud customers, the value shows in engagement quality: open rates and click-through rates that reflect individual relevance rather than list-wide broadcast, improved campaign ROI through AI-optimized delivery, and loyalty program engagement that reflects relationship depth rather than transaction volume.

Across both, the compounding effect of unified data and orchestrated decisioning is what transforms hyper-personalization from a POC into a sustained growth mechanism — one that gets measurably better over time.

 

Next in the Series

In our next post, we will examine how the SAP Advanced Success Plan for Customer Experience addresses Unified Customer Data and the challenge of breaking down silos across marketing, commerce, sales, and service. Stay tuned.

entry.title: KI-Kompetenz aufbauen durch Peer-Learning: Formate
entry.content:
ChangeWorkout Camp 2026 | Workshop-Zusammenfassung

Beim ChangeWorkout Camp 2026 habe ich einen Workshop zu einem Thema gemacht, das mich lange beschäftigt: Wie baut man KI-Kompetenz in Organisationen auf - nachhaltig, aktionsorientiert? Was folgt, ist eine Zusammenfassung der Präsentation & Diskussion. 

Warum Peer-Learning bei KI funktioniert

KI-Adoption scheitert selten an fehlendem Technologieverständnis. Sie scheitert daran, dass Menschen sich nicht trauen, etwas auszuprobieren. Wer in einer Gruppe Gleichgesinnter lernt, fühlt sich sicher genug, um Fehler zu machen. Das ist der Kern.

Ich habe das mit Claude recherchiert und in ein Modell überführt: Psychologische Sicherheit, soziales Lernen, Kompetenzerleben und Selbstwirksamkeit erklären, warum Peer-Formate bei KI gut funktionieren. Community of Practice und Community of Interest verschmelzen dabei zu etwas Eigenem. 

Die vier Peer-Learning Formate in der KI Transformation

Promptathon

Zwei bis acht Stunden, echte Arbeitsprobleme, kaum Theorie. Teams von drei bis fünf Personen lösen Challenges aus ihrem Arbeitskontext auf einem digitalen Whiteboard, begleitet von Coaches. Wir haben das beim ChangeWorkout Camp 2025 gemacht, bei SAP intern mit hunderten Teilnehmenden in Breakout-Sessions.

Das Entscheidende: Die Challenges kommen von den Teilnehmenden selbst. Wer seine eigene Realität mitbringt, lernt nachhaltig. Das Ergebnis ist mehr Handlungskompetenz für KI Tools. Fehler werden nicht versteckt, sondern gefeiert.

Was mich immer wieder überrascht: In allen Firmen gibt es Kolleginnen und Kollegen, die KI-Tools kaum nutzen. Nach zwei Stunden Promptathon sieht das anders aus. ISt natürlich nur ein erster Schritt.

Lernzirkel

Diese sind nachhaltiger als ein einmaliges Event, aber aufwändiger. Ich kenne zwei Varianten, es gibt natürlich viele Mischformen:

Geführte Lernzirkel laufen über 2-3 Workshops à sechs Stunden, ergänzt durch Coachings, über zwölf Monate in einer Community. Wir starten mit einem Promptathon, gehen dann in Use-Case-Exploration mit Design-Thinking-Methodik und enden je nach Gruppe mit Prototypenentwicklung oder Strategiearbeit. Das Format läuft im Rahmen des New Learning Lab.

Selbstgesteuerte Lernzirkel mit LernOS laufen über drei bis fünf Personen, eine Stunde pro Woche, drei Monate. Jede Woche liest jeder die Grundlagen im Leitfaden, macht Übung , in der Gruppe wird dann reflektiert. Kein Trainer ist hier nötig. Der LernOS-Leitfaden für KI ist Open Source, als PDF und Markdown verfügbar, Creative Commons lizenziert.

Hier liegt aber auch die Herausforderung: Selbstorganisation ist schwierig. Bei unseren Durchläufen hatten wir über fünfzig Prozent Drop-off schon in der ersten Session und davor. Von über 200 Angemeldeten blieben viele nicht dabei. Wer keine Verpflichtung hat, priorisiert anders. Das ist kein Versagen des Formats, sondern eine kulturelle Realität, die man einplanen muss.

Eine Möglichkeit ist die sogenannte Learning Circle Experience mit Kick-off und Zwischenstopps, damit Gruppen nicht allein gelassen werden. Das senkt den Drop-off.

Hackathon

Das Beispiel von SAP lief so: Fünf Vollzeittage, cross-funktionale Teams mit drei bis sechs Personen. Das sind Business-Vertreter, technische Profile, manchmal externe Berater. Teams bewerben sich dann mit einem konkreten Use Case, der von einer Jury geprüft wird. Nicht jeder Use Case wird angenommen: Wenn es ein KI Standardprodukt wie Agent, Assistent, Workflow etc. auf der Plattform gibt, macht ein Hackathon keinen Sinn.

Was am Ende rauskommt, ist kein Lernprodukt, sondern ein funktionierender MVP. Bei den Basler Verkehrsbetrieben wurde ein Reparaturprozess für Busse optimiert, der vorher oft lange liegen blieb. Bei Viessmann sagte der Lagerleiter am Ende: "Ich wusste gar nicht, was ein Hackathon ist, aber jetzt habe ich keine Angst mehr vor KI." 

Wichtig: Die Entwicklungsumgebung muss nah an der Produktionsumgebung sein. oder direkt am besten im Produktivsystem sein. Wer zwei von fünf Tagen mit Datenzugriffen und Integrationsproblemen verbringt, baut keinen Prototypen. Das muss vorher gelöst sein.

Der "Sweet Spot" des Hackathons ist ein Use Case zu viel für einen Freitagnachmittag, zu wenig für ein richtiges Projekt. Hier ein Artikel auch mit Beispielen von Use Cases. KI-Hackathons als Innovationsmotor: von der Idee zum MVP

Communities im Rahmen von KI

Communities sind kein Selbstzweck. Das war einer der Punkte in unserer Diskussion. Wer eine interne KI-Community aufbaut, ohne zu klären, was das Ziel ist, bekommt eine nette Runde, die sich trifft und redet. Das ist nicht wertlos, aber es skaliert nicht.

Bei SAP haben wir intern die Experience Garage mit kleinen Challenges und eine Community für KI-Ambassadoren, extern eine Community für Change & Transformations Professionals mit monatlichen Coffee-Corner-Sessions, Blogs und Webinaren. Was funktioniert: ein fester Rhythmus, eine kuratierende Person und Impulse aus der Praxis. 

Die 1-Prozent-Regel gilt auch hier: In jeder Community macht nur ein Prozent der Mitglieder aktiv eigene Inhalte. Der Rest konsumiert. Das ist normal. Man darf sich davon nicht frustrieren lassen. 

Die Pilotfalle

Das war die Frage im Workshop die wir am Ende diskutierten. Viele Unternehmen machen einen Piloten, oder nur Trainings und was passiert dann? 

Meine Antwort war: Es gibt kein Patentrezept. Was hilft, ist eine Kombination aus Formaten, die aufeinander aufbauen. Promptathon als Einstieg, Lernzirkel für Vertiefung, Community für Verstetigung. Und es braucht jemanden, der das kuratiert: einen Community-Gärtner, der aktiv Verbindungen herstellt zwischen Lösungen und Problemen, zwischen Piloten und Interessierten.

Und wenn Unternehmen wollen, dass Mitarbeitende ein bestimmtes KI-Skill-Level erreichen, weil es für die Zukunft wichtig ist, dann reicht freiwilliges Peer-Learning allein nicht. Dann braucht es Verbindlichkeit und Ressourcen und klare Ziele. 

KI-Agenten als Kuratoren

Das Thema am Ende des Workshops, das bei mir hängen geblieben ist war dann die Idee: Ein Agent sammelt in der Community geteilte Lösungen, Probleme und Erfahrungen. Wenn jemand ein Problem eingibt, prüft der Agent, ob jemand anderes etwas Ähnliches gelöst hat, und stellt die Verbindung her. Kein Newsletter, den niemand liest. Kein Channel, in dem man nicht weiß, was relevant ist. Ich werde versuchen, daran mit Community Teilnehmenden zu arbeiten.

Aussagen und O-Töne

"Lernen darf nie Selbstzweck sein. Die beste Anwendung ist, wenn du konkrete Probleme in deiner Arbeitspraxis löst."

"Ganz selbstgesteuert lernen, da sind viele irritiert. Was, es gibt niemanden, der uns die Sachen erklärt?"

"Eine Community braucht einen Gärtner, einen Moderator. Jemanden, der kuratiert und z.B. rausfindet, welche Lösungen sich auf konkrete Probleme anderer anwenden lassen."

 

Praxis-Tipps

1. Challenges vorab abfragen
Beim Promptathon kommen die besten Ergebnisse, wenn die Challenges aus dem Arbeitsalltag der Teilnehmenden stammen. Generische Aufgaben funktionieren als Kompromiss, rollenspezifische Challenges erzeugen mehr Transfer.

2. Fehler feiern
Wer im Promptathon einen schlechten Prompt baut, lernt mehr als wer nichts ausprobiert. Das muss explizit kommuniziert werden.

3. Drop-off einplanen
Bei selbstgesteuerten Lernzirkeln ist ein Drop-off von über fünfzig Prozent normal. Wer das als Scheitern verbucht, zieht die falschen Schlüsse. 

4. Hackathon-Umgebung nah an der Produktion halten
Datenzugang und Integration müssen im Vorfeld klar sein. Und dann sollte man Probleme aus dem Arbeitsalltag lösen.

5. Gewinner mit Ressourcen belohnen
Implementierungsbudget oder gesponsorte Projektstunden für den nächsten OKR-Zyklus sind wirksamer als Trophäen. Sie signalisieren: Das ist ernst gemeint.

6. Community-Gärtner benennen
Jede Community braucht eine Person, die aktiv Verbindungen herstellt. Bei der Telekom gibt es jemanden, der durch die Organisation läuft und Leute anspricht, die etwas zu teilen haben. Das ist eine Kurationsstrategie, keine Moderationsaufgabe.

7. Formate kombinieren
Promptathon als Einstieg, Lernzirkel für Vertiefung, Community für Verstetigung. Einzelne Events erzeugen Energie, aber keine nachhaltige Veränderung. 

Resümee

Peer-Learning ist kein Ersatz für strategische KI-Einführung, aber es ist der menschliche Klebstoff, der Adoption und Transformation weiter bringt. Top-Down wie Zielsetzung ist wichtig, PEer-Learning kann bei der Buttom-Up Transformation helfen.

 

Ressourcen

LernOS KI-Leitfaden (Open Source, CC BY 4.0)

New Learning Lab: KI für die Personalentwicklung

SAP Learning & Change Community auf LinkedIn

SAP Business AI Community

Blog: KI-Kompetenzentwicklung und Weiterbildung, Praxiserfahrungen und bewährte Ansätze

Blog: Mehr als nur Technologie: Wie menschzentrierte KI Akzeptanz und Mehrwert schafft

Blog: SAP AI Skills Assessment Results, SAP 2026

Adopt Change Transform Podcast auf Spotify

Adopt Change Transform Podcast auf Apple Podcasts

LinkedIn-Artikel: Hackathons als Innovationsmotor und Antreiber von AI-Transformationen

Monatliche virtuelle Kaffee-Ecke rund um Learning, Change, Adoption

entry.title: Re: ST-PI Patch 31 not showing retrofit use case in trans...
entry.content:
Hi Shivani,
In addition to ST-PI 7.4 SP31 being installed, there are notes that are required, which deliver the Retrofit functionality.  Check https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/setup-administration/setting-up-managed-systems#setting-up-retrofit -> You've installed at least ST-PI 740 SP31 and implemented SAP Note 3688071 Information published on SAP site and 3639096 Information published on SAP site on your development systems of your implementation and maintenance track.

The document also lists other requirements that you must ensure are met.  I hope this is helpful to your setup process.

Best regards,
Paula

entry.title: Build a custom logic for Reclasification in Group reporting
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

entry.title: Re: Function module RSDMD_API_DELETE does not delete
entry.content:
Hi Britta

According the type group RSDMD, e_result = 6 is rsdmd_c_no_data_but_go_on:

CONSTANTS:
  rsdmd_c_all_deleted       TYPE i VALUE 0,
  rsdmd_c_parts_deleted     TYPE i VALUE 1,
  rsdmd_c_bg_started        TYPE i VALUE 2,
  rsdmd_c_no_data           TYPE i VALUE 3,
  rsdmd_c_none_deleted      TYPE i VALUE 4,
  rsdmd_c_error             TYPE i VALUE 5,
  rsdmd_c_no_data_but_go_on TYPE i VALUE 6, 

which most probably means, that with the given value there is nothing to be deleted.

I would suggest to debug the deletion. Internally the program RSDMDD_DELETE_KERNEL is called by RSDMDD_DELETE_MASTER_DATA to do the actual deletion. 

br

Roland

entry.title: SAP BO Publication Dynamic Recipients error to the SFTP Destination
entry.content:
Hello Experts,
We get an error while scheduling the publication

We see that the Publication schedule with dynamic recipients fails with "exception was caught while fetching dynamic recipients data" error to the SFTP destination after upgrading to BI 4.3

 

Environment Details:

SAP BO version: BI 4.3 SP05 Patch 300 (three node cluster)

OS version: RedHat 9.6 Plow x86_64

Error Message: An exception was caught while fetching dynamic recipients data.  Did you properly configure your publication? (FBE60311)

  • The error is seen only while scheduling to the SFTP destination using dynamic recipients, it works fine when scheduling to Email or other destinations.
  • The individual WebI report runs fine, the report used for source and recipient list for this publication runs fine.
  • The universe and connections (secured) are working fine.

Steps taken till now:

  • Checked by manually selecting “Select recipients from list” under the Dynamic Recipients, and checking “One database fetch per recipient” under Report Bursting Method, didn’t help
  • Found the below errors in the logs, manually recreating both the Source document and the Dynamic Recipient list, didn’t help
    • instance [177029] may not have any files associated with it. Reason: com.crystaldecisions.sdk.exception.SDKException$PropertyNotFound: The property with ID SI_FILES does not exist in the object (FWM 02021) at com.crystaldecisions.sdk.occa.infostore.internal.InfoObject.getFileLocales(InfoObject.java:1437)
  • The source document and dynamic recipient list are mapped correctly in the Personalization
  • The issue is seen only while scheduling with dynamic recipients bursting, scheduling to enterprise recipients (with profiles) is working fine.
  • The Destination_AllowList.txt file is already set with the destination under config folder
  • Tried to enable "Refresh on Open", and then "Purge" the dynamic recipients, didn't help
  • Tried to replicate the issue in one of the test servers (Windows OS) from the same version, but couldn't replicate the issue

Found the below exception in the logs while the error message is seen,

  • Caused by: com.businessobjects.sdk.core.CoreException: findBundleConfigurator problem with URL: jar:file:/tmp/lwja-BF43196C-A8AF-470D-EF79-8283A79A950D/lwja.jar!/META-INF/MANIFEST.MF 
    at com.businessobjects.sdk.core.internal.dynenv.impl.ManifestDiscoveryManager.findBundleConfigurator(ManifestDiscoveryManager.java:173)
  •  Caused by: java.io.FileNotFoundException: /tmp/lwja-BF43196C-A8AF-470D-EF79-8283A79A950D/lwja.jar (error 2 - No such file or directory)
    at java.util.zip.ZipFile.open(Native Method)

We think some required files are not generated in the /tmp folder.

However, the Linux OS root /tmp have all the required privileges for the BO service account. Tried to create a new /tmp location and update this new /tmp for the APS Publication service, but didn't help.

We noticed that no files are generated in the APS Publication service (./java/pjs/container/temp) after restart or while scheduling the publication
Can you please assist?

Regards,
Girinaath

entry.title: Microsoft Teams as a Delivery Channel for My Metrics Reports in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Analytics Cloud has continuously evolved its alerting and monitoring capabilities for the My Metrics List feature. Users can already receive scheduled report notifications through two established channels: the SAC Notification Center and email inbox. These channels ensure that business users stay informed about metric movements on a schedule that fits their workflow.

With the 2026 Q2 release, SAP Analytics Cloud extends this capability further by introducing Microsoft Teams as a supported delivery channel. Business users can now receive their My Metrics report notifications directly within Teams — the collaboration hub where they already spend a significant portion of their working day. This eliminates the need to switch between applications to access critical metric updates and act on them quickly.

Feature Capabilities

The Microsoft Teams delivery channel is designed to be fully consistent with the existing notification scope available today via email reports and the SAC Notification Center. The following capabilities are included:

  • Channel Configuration:  Users can select Microsoft Teams as a notification option when configuring their Metrics Report subscription. The option is available within the Distribution List step of the subscription wizard.
  • Teams Channel Email Integration:  The integration leverages Teams channel email addresses (e.g. <channelname>@<tenant>.teams.ms). Users enter the channel email into the 'Send Emails to Microsoft Teams Channel' dialog — no additional connector or app installation is required.  For sending an email to channel in Microsoft Teams please refer to here
  • Notification Content:  Each Teams notification includes all standard report details: Metrics List Name, Subscription Name, Recurrence Pattern, Start Date, End Date, an Overview of Metrics table with Latest Value, Changes in Value, Previous Value, and Latest Variance — plus clickable metric name links and a direct 'Open My Metrics' deep-link to SAC.
  • Scheduling and Trigger Respect:  Teams notifications honour existing scheduling configurations and trigger conditions set during subscription setup. No separate scheduling is needed for the Teams channel.
  • Reply in Thread:  Teams thread functionality is preserved — users can reply inline to a notification post, enabling quick discussion or follow-up directly in context.

Getting Started

Configuration is completed directly within the existing 'Subscribe to Metrics Report' wizard in SAP Analytics Cloud. No additional plugins, connectors, or admin setup are required beyond having the Teams channel email address available.

  1. Open the Subscription Wizard  From your My Metrics list, click the options menu (⋯) on any metric card and select 'Subscribe'. The 'Subscribe to Metrics Report' dialog opens. Open Subscription Dialog

     

     

  2. Configure the Schedule  In Step 2, set the Recurrence Pattern (e.g. Daily), the Start Date of First Occurrence, and the End Date. Use 'Check Availability' to validate the schedule.Define Occurence

     

     

  3. Add the Teams Channel as a Recipient  In Step 3 (Distribution List), click '+ Add Recipients', then select 'Send Emails to Microsoft Teams Channel'. Enter the Teams channel email address (available from Teams under Get email address for the channel) and click Save.Add email address of Team channel

     

     

  4. Save the Subscription  Once the Teams channel email is added to the Recipients list (alongside any individual user recipients), click Save. The subscription is now active and will deliver to both the Teams channel and any individual recipients.Save the subscription report

     

     

  5. Metrics Report in Microsoft TeamsMetrics Report in Teams Channel

     

     

     

Summary and Outlook

With the 2026 Q2 release, SAP Analytics Cloud adds Microsoft Teams as a delivery channel for My Metrics reports. Users can now receive scheduled notifications — including subscription details, a full metrics overview, and a direct link to SAC — directly in Teams, alongside the existing SAC Notification Center and email options. Setup requires no additional integration; users simply add a Teams channel email address in the existing subscription wizard.

 A lot more is coming for My Metrics and Metrics Report, and I'm excited about what's ahead. Stay tuned for updates in the near future. Until then, I'd love to hear your thoughts — leave a comment, share your thoughts. That conversation is what keeps us pointed in the right direction.

entry.title: Variable Pay-Requirement-Two different bonus target percentages
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

                 I am working on a new requirement from a customer where they use 2 different bonus target percentages one is called company based target percentage , which is very much like target bonus percentage but other one is Individual incentive percentage. Both the values are specific to an employee and can vary during the year based on various conditions like job level, location, manager etc it's maintained by the admins. 

Overall requirement shared by business 

Total Annual Variable Pay Amount = Employee's Annual Base Salary × [Component Tracks] × Time Factor × Individual Performance Multiplier
  • Track 1: Target % of Company based target × Business Score
  • Track 2: Target % of Annual Individual Performance Incentive
Example Table
Annual Base Salary Company based target: Target % Company based target: Business Score Annual Individual Performance Incentive: Target % Individual Performance Multiplier
Annual Base Salary 100,000Company based Target 8.0%Business Score Global: 110%Individual performance incentive 12.5%Individual performance evaluation 90%
     
Assuming employee was eligible for full year 100%
track1- Company Based Target
  • Calculation: 100,000 X 8% X 110% X 100% X 90%
  • Total: 7,920
Track2- Annual Individual Performance Incentive
  • Calculation: 100,000 X 12.5% X 100% X 90%
  • Total: 11,250
Grand Total Payout
  • 7,920 + 11,250 = 19,170

 In variable pay setup , I can understand we can try to set it up using 2 different business plans but we do not have option to setup 2 separate basis calculation formulas. Has someone encountered such a need, especially for customers from China? would be great to know the thoughts from the experts.

 @xavierlegarrec - Your thoughts on this would be appreciated.

SAP SuccessFactors Compensation

entry.title: 4-Eyes by Configuration: Multi-Processor Workflow Approvals in SAP S/4HANA 2608
entry.content:
Introduction

A quick note before diving in: this blog was initially drafted with the help of SAP Joule for Consultants. — a great example of how AI tools can meaningfully accelerate the writing process.  And improved with Claude Sonnet. That said, all content, accuracy, and opinions here are entirely my own responsibility.

A special thank you to the colleagues who built and shipped these features under significant time pressure. Great software doesn’t happen without great teamwork — and it ultimately takes dedicated engineers to turn ideas into delivered, production-ready capabilities.

In today’s compliance-driven enterprise landscape, ensuring that critical business decisions are never made unilaterally is both a governance imperative and, in many industries, a regulatory requirement. Workflow automation has long been a cornerstone of SAP’s approach to streamlining approvals — but automation without embedded controls can introduce new risks rather than eliminate them.

Two foundational control mechanisms address this challenge directly:

  • Multi-Processor Task Completion — ensuring that a defined number of independent individuals must each process a task before a workflow can continue
  • Approver Exclusion (4-Eyes Principle) — ensuring that the person who created a business document cannot also approve it (for details on this capability, see the linked resource)

With SAP S/4HANA 2608 (and 2702 for customers on that release cycle), SAP has introduced both of these capabilities natively within the Manage Workflow application and the broader flexible workflow framework — empowering Business Process Experts to configure robust dual-control and multi-control approval processes without writing a single line of code.

But governance is only as strong as its weakest configuration. That is why this release also introduces a new Validation Callback method that prevents misconfigured workflow definitions from ever being activated — closing the gap between documentation-based guidance and system-enforced compliance.

This blog covers all three features comprehensively: what they are, why they were introduced, how they work, and how to configure them correctly.

Background & Motivation

The Governance Gap in Workflow Automation

Prior to these enhancements, the Manage Workflows application offered two task completion modes for workflow step recipients:

Mode Runtime Behavior Limitation

One recipientA single shared task instance is created; the first person to act completes the stepNo guarantee of independent review
All recipientsA separate task instance is created for each recipient; every recipient must complete their taskInflexible — requires full group participation with no middle ground

Neither mode offered the precision required for a true four-eyes or six-eyes principle. Organizations needing exactly two or three independent completions had to rely on the "run with previous" feature combined with manual process discipline or documentation-based guidance — all of which are fragile controls that do not scale in enterprise environments.

Multi-Processor Task Completion (4-Eyes / 6-Eyes via Configuration)

What Is It?

The Multi-Processor Task Completion feature addresses this gap directly: it ensures that a defined number of independent individuals each complete their own task instance before the workflow continues.

This is the mechanism that enables a true four-eyes principle (2 processors) or six-eyes principle (3 processors) in scenarios where multiple parallel approvers must each independently act — not just one of them, and not necessarily all of them.

Task Instance Behavior: The Three Modes

Completion mode is configured in the Recipients section of a workflow step using a radio button group:

Mode Runtime Behavior

One recipientA single shared task instance is created; the first recipient to act completes the step
Individual task per recipient — AllA multi-instance task is created; all recipients must complete the task
Two or three recipientsA multi-instance task is created; the defined number of recipients must approve, or one must reject

The two or three recipients option is the key addition in releases 2608/2702. It fills the gap between "one person acts" and "everyone must act" — enabling precise control over how many independent completions are required before the workflow proceeds.

Recipient Assignment Types

The recipient configuration UI is used consistently across steps, notifications, and other workflow artifacts. Supported assignment types include:

  • Agent Rule — dynamically resolves recipients based on business rules
  • Team — assigns the task to a predefined team (configured via Manage Teams and Responsibilities)
  • Concrete User — directly specifies one or more named users

Note: If you select the two or three recipients mode but assign only one concrete user, the system will display a validation error.

 Runtime Design

Several new UI elements have been introduced to support transparency at runtime:

  • A status bar at the task instance level shows who is currently processing a task within a team — and clearly indicates who has already approved.

 

  • The workflow instance component now includes an improved status bar displaying the number of required processors versus the number who have already acted.

     These additions give both approvers and process administrators real-time visibility into the state of multi-processor steps.

The Uniqueness Rule: One Person, One Completion

A critical safeguard of this feature is that the same person cannot complete more than one parallel task instance. This is what makes the four-eyes and six-eyes principles meaningful — it is not sufficient for two task instances to exist; they must be completed by two different people.

This uniqueness rule applies even in edge cases involving substitution and forwarding:

Scenario Behavior

SubstitutionIf Person A completes a task as a substitute for Person B, Person A cannot also complete their own parallel task instance
ForwardingIf Person A forwards their task to Person C, and Person C already has their own parallel instance, the uniqueness rule still applies
DelegationThe initiator cannot act as a delegatee for any parallel task instance in the same step

Resolution for blocked users: If a person is unable to complete their own task because the uniqueness rule applies (e.g., they already acted via substitution on a parallel instance), they can — where permitted — forward their own task instance to another eligible person. This self-service resolution requires no administrative intervention.

An explicit error message is displayed when a user attempts to complete a second task instance in violation of this rule.

Validation Callback for Mandatory Step Configurations

The Problem It Solves

The introduction of the N-processor option increases the risk that a workflow modeler will inadvertently misconfigure a step that must always be set to "All of the recipients." The "Verify Intercompany General Journal Entry" step is the primary example — but the pattern applies to any scenario where a specific completion mode is a non-negotiable compliance requirement.

Previously, this requirement was enforced only through documentation. With the 2608 release, a validation callback mechanism now prevents the activation of a workflow definition if a mandatory step is incorrectly configured — catching errors before they affect live business processes.

How the Validation Works

The validation is implemented via the if_swf_flex_ifs_def_appl~on_validation_callback method. When a workflow modeler attempts to activate a workflow definition, the system executes this callback. If the VerifyMultiInstance step is not configured to require all processors (cv_all_processors), the system raises a validation exception and blocks activation.

This represents a significant improvement over documentation-only guidance:

  • It operates at activation time, not at runtime — catching errors before they affect live processes
  • It is automatic and non-bypassable — a modeler cannot accidentally skip the validation
  • It provides a clear error message directing the modeler to correct the configuration
  • It scales — the same pattern can be applied to any scenario where specific configurations are mandatory

Code Reference (Baseline for Implementation)

The following ABAP snippet serves as a baseline for implementing the validation callback:

METHOD if_swf_flex_ifs_def_appl~on_validation_callback.  
DATA(lt_activity) = io_workflow->get_all_activities( ).  
" Raise a validation error if the multi-instance step is not configured to require all processors.  
LOOP AT lt_activity INTO DATA(lo_activity).    
IF lo_activity->get_step_id( ) = 'VerifyMultiInstance'.      
DATA(lv_number_of_proc) = lo_activity->get_number_of_requ_processors( ).      
IF lv_number_of_proc <> lo_activity->cv_all_processors.        
RAISE EXCEPTION TYPE cx_swf_flex_ifs_exception          
MESSAGE ID 'Your Message Class'
TYPE 'E'          
NUMBER 'Your Message Number'.      
ENDIF.    
ENDIF.  
ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

Scope and Intentional Boundaries

It is important to understand the deliberate scope boundaries of this feature:

In Scope Out of Scope

Validation at activation time that prevents an invalid workflow definition from being activatedA UX-level change that hides or disables invalid configuration options in the UI (e.g., greying out the "2 processors" option for the VerifyMultiInstance step)

Discussions are already underway to explore UX improvements that would disable invalid options directly in the configuration interface.

Combined Use & Real-World Scenarios

When to Use Which Feature

Understanding when to apply each feature — and how they complement each other — is key to designing effective compliance-oriented workflows:

Requirement Recommended Feature

Prevent the initiator from approving their own requestExcluded Agents
Require exactly 2 independent people to each complete a taskTwo recipients mode
Require exactly 3 independent people to each complete a taskThree recipients mode
Require all members of a group to complete a taskAll recipients mode
Prevent a specific step from being misconfigured at activationValidation Callback
Combine initiator exclusion with multi-processor completion4-Eyes Principle + Multi-Processor (both active simultaneously)

In many real-world scenarios, all three features will be active simultaneously — delivering layered, system-enforced governance across the full approval lifecycle.

Example: Financial Processes — Journal Entry Postings

For multi-level verification scenarios — such as a 4-level approval hierarchy based on distinct monetary thresholds across multiple company codes — workflows can be defined with sequentially ordered steps, each with its own amount-based precondition and assigned approver group. The multi-processor task completion feature ensures that each level of that hierarchy meets the required independent review count.

Configuration Checklist & Getting Started

Prerequisites and System Requirements

  • SAP S/4HANA 2608 (on-premise) or 2702 (for customers on that release cycle)
  • Flexible Workflow must be active and configured for the relevant business object
  • Users must be set up as business users with appropriate authorizations
  • The Manage Teams and Responsibilities application is recommended for agent determination
  • For scenario developers: the Validation Callback is implemented via the on_validation_callback method

Best Practices

  • Always test your workflow configuration using the Simulation feature (available from SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2408 onwards) — verify which workflow definition triggers for a given business object instance before going live
  • Explicitly configure excluded agents to avoid workflow stalls or compliance gaps when the initiator is the only eligible approver
  • Define clear preconditions for each step in multi-step scenarios to avoid unintended overlaps in approval routing

entry.title: Re: Variable Pay-Requirement-Two different bonus target percentages
entry.content: First, it seems your business calculation also includes the personal. This need being very specific to the individual employee is not a common request. Most times, there are variations in business section weight vs. Individual section weight but it is based on data such as job level or pay grade. My first thought is to push back a little and see if there is any way to standardize some of the percentages to make the configuration based on something other than employee id. If that does not work, because the calculation seems to be formulaic and does not have discretion, I might try to configure as a base X business plan with many custom fields using ALCF to complete the calculations

entry.title: Re: While doing MIRO business got the error negative vaule
entry.content:
If your MIRO document is parked (so not 'as already posted') but posting fails due to this error, check:

  • Stock quantity and stock movements to confirm actual available stock.
  • Whether relevant SAP Notes/Support Packages for your system version have been applied that address this issue.
  • That pricing and quantities on the invoice match the stock situation.

Check also:

  • 3123287 - MIRO: Moving average price for material would be negative ( Part 1) 
  • 3667067 - Negative Moving Average Price - Enhancements for Error Message M8 783

entry.title: Re: Validation to block cancellation of AR invoice for sp...
entry.content:
This version is for HANA and only allows the “manager” user to cancel

IF :object_type in ('13', '17', '23') AND :transaction_type = 'A' THEN

    DECLARE temp_var_0 INT := 0;

    SELECT COUNT(B."DocEntry") INTO temp_var_0
    FROM "OINV" B
    LEFT OUTER JOIN "OUSR" C ON B."UserSign" = C."USERID"
    WHERE B."CANCELED" IN ('C','Y')
      AND C."U_NAME" NOT IN ('manager')
      AND B."DocEntry" = :list_of_cols_val_tab_del;

    IF temp_var_0 > 0 THEN
        error := -1099;
        error_message := 'No tiene autorizacion para Cancelar documentos, llame a Gerencia';
    END IF;

END IF;

entry.title: Re: Unable to Remove House Bank Assignment – Error "House...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. 

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

Solution for Reassigning Bank Accounts to New House Banks

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, if a house bank account is already in use, it cannot be directly deleted or reassigned due to existing dependencies such as accounting documents, payment programs, or bank statements. However, you can follow these steps to achieve your business requirement:

  1. Close the Current Bank Accounts:

    • You need to close the existing bank accounts that are linked to House Bank 9983. This involves sending a closing request and confirming the closure in the system.

  2. Create New Bank Accounts:

    • After closing the existing accounts, you can create new bank accounts and assign them to the newly created house banks.

  3. Copy Bank Account Data:

    • You can copy the data from the closed bank accounts to the new ones to ensure continuity in your financial operations.

  4. Update Configuration Settings:

    • Ensure that all relevant configuration settings are updated to reflect the new house bank assignments. This includes updating payment programs and any other settings that reference the old house bank accounts.

  5. Use SSCUI for Message Control:

    • If you encounter error messages during this process, you can change these messages to warnings using the SSCUI ‘Change Message Control for Bank Account Management’ (ID 103894). This allows you to bypass certain validations temporarily.

For detailed steps on closing and reopening bank accounts, refer to the SAP documentation on Closing Bank Accounts. Cash and Liquidity Management - Closing Bank Accounts

Important Considerations

  • Ensure that all transactions related to the old house bank accounts are completed before initiating the closure.
  • Be aware that linking multiple house bank accounts to a single bank account can lead to unintended side effects in certain apps.

By following these steps, you can effectively reassign your bank accounts to new house banks while maintaining compliance with SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud’s operational constraints.

For more details, see the following information:

[1] Error messages indicating the deletion/change of house bank account assigned in a bank account is not possible
[2] Set Up bank determination for Payment Transactions not possible to delete error
[3] Cash and Liquidity Management - Closing Bank Accounts

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Clarification on MRP Live (MD01N) Behavior for P V Se...
entry.content:
Here are answers to your questions on MRP Live (MD01N) Production Version determination and BOM explosion:

  1. Prerequisite conditions for Production Version determination and BOM explosion
  • A valid Production Version must exist in C223 with a BOM and routing assigned.
  • The validity period of the Production Version (MKAL‑ADATU/BDATU) must cover the MRP requirement date.
  • The BOM itself must be valid on the same requirement date.
  • Lot size and plant assignments must be consistent.
  • Without a valid Production Version, MRP Live cannot perform BOM explosion.
  1. Which date is used for validity checks?
  • The system uses the MRP requirement date (PBED‑PBDAT) to validate both the Production Version and BOM.
  • It does not use:
    • Purchase Requisition Delivery Date (EBAN‑LFDAT)
    • Purchase Requisition Release Date (EBAN‑FRGDT)
    • Purchase Requisition Creation Date (EBAN‑BADAT)
  • The requirement date generated during MRP planning is the decisive factor.
  1. Standard Documentation
  • SAP Note 2267880 – S4TWL – BOM, Routing, Production Version: Explains that Production Versions are mandatory in S/4HANA MRP Live and BOM explosion is only possible through them.
  • Transaction MD_MRP_FORCE_CLASSIC: Provides logs and diagnostic information when MRP Live cannot determine a valid Production Version or BOM.

So the Summary is MRP Live checks Production Version and BOM validity against the MRP requirement date (PBED‑PBDAT). If validity fails, you’ll see messages like “No valid production version found” or “No valid BOM”. To avoid incomplete PRs, ensure Production Versions and BOMs are valid for the planning horizon and correctly maintained in C223.

entry.title: Re: Invoice summery can not be sent to customer via e-mail
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. 

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

Output Channel Error

When you encounter an error stating that the system does not support output channel ‘00010’, it typically means that the selected channel is not configured or supported for the specific output type you are trying to use. In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, the supported output channels are: 

  • EMAIL: Allows flexible configuration of senders and recipients as well as the use of email templates.
  • PRINT: Uses the SAP spool system to print documents.
  • EXTOM: Sends raw data from the standard output process to an external system.
  • EDI: Sends electronic messages to business receivers.

If ‘00010’ is not one of these supported channels, you will need to select a valid channel for your output type. You can configure the output channels in the ‘Output Parameter Determination’ app to ensure the correct channel is selected for your output type.

For more details, see the following information:

Manage Your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - Output Channels

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: DRC for India GSTR reports
entry.content:
Hi all,

Please note that basic statutory reporting features are included with SAP S/4HANA Cloud. However, advanced statutory reporting and compliance capabilities require an additional SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC) license.

The license material SAP Document and Reporting Compliance S4HC (8013658) is associated with scope item 5XU, which provides additional DRC functionality. For more information about scope item 5XU, please refer to the following link:

https://me.sap.com/processnavigator/SolS/EARL_SolS-013/2602/SolP/5XU?region=IN

For confirmation of the licenses purchased in your landscape and for any licensing-related questions, please contact your SAP Account Manager.

 

Additionally, configuration activity '101071 – Setting Up Your Statutory Reporting' is required for statutory reporting setup (for example, for an India reporting entity). This configuration activity is dependent on scope item 1J2.

 

Kindly refer the below help document for more information about the India GSTR reports,

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/634261119fec4d58970471f2c4a9a740/b9524d0554714ab6b0dacc8866c0fac9.html?version=LATEST&locale=en-US

 

Best regards,

Atanu

SAP Support - S/4HANA Cloud

entry.title: Re: What’s New in SAP HANA Cloud – March 2026
entry.content:
@thomashammer 

This blog is the March 2026 release overview for SAP HANA Cloud. It covers key updates across AI agents, modeling, administration, and performance.

Highlights:

  • Discovery Agent + Data Agent (beta): natural-language data discovery and execution using a database object knowledge graph.

  • Semantic onboarding of calculation views into SAP Business Data Cloud: reuse existing HANA Cloud models without data movement.

  • Performance Analysis app in SAP HANA Cloud Central: guided workflows for CPU, memory, lock issues, plus an on-demand chart library.

  • Text classification improvements in the predictive analysis library: better performance, efficiency, and automated ML for text features.

  • Elastic Compute Node policy: new ECN policy for better management and control of compute resources.

  • Additional ML enhancements (feature agglomeration, affinity propagation clustering) and data center expansions.

entry.title: Re: [S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition] Multi-approver workflo...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. 

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

Multi-Approver Workflow for Supplier Invoices by Cost Center in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

To implement a multi-approver workflow for supplier invoices based on cost centers in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you can follow these steps:

1. Standard Mechanism for Multi-Approver Setup

Currently, the standard mechanism in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition does not directly support assigning multiple approvers based on cost centers for supplier invoices. The ‘Responsible Person’ field in cost center master data only allows a single person to be assigned.

2. Using ‘Manage Teams and Responsibilities’ App

The ‘Manage Teams and Responsibilities’ app can be used to define teams and assign functions to team members,  but it does not directly map to supplier invoice approval against a cost center. This app is typically used for defining roles and responsibilities in other contexts, such as journal entry verification.

3. Workflow Configuration for Supplier Invoices

You can configure flexible workflows for supplier invoices using the ‘Manage Workflows for Supplier Invoices’ app.  This app allows you to define condition-based workflows for approval processes, including multi-step approvals. However, the configuration for cost center-based approval is not directly supported.

4. Guidance and Documentation

For more detailed guidance, you can refer to the SAP Help Portal and community blogs that discuss flexible workflows and their configurations. These resources can provide insights into how to set up workflows for supplier invoices, although they may not cover cost center-specific scenarios directly.

If you need a custom solution, you might consider using extensibility options or consulting with SAP experts to explore potential customizations or workarounds.

Conclusion

While the standard S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition does not directly support multi-approver workflows for supplier invoices based on cost centers, you can explore flexible workflows and team configurations for other types of approvals. For specific requirements, custom solutions may be necessary.

For more details, see the following information:

A Configuration Example of How to Optimize Journal Entry Verification

Flexible Workflow for Supplier Invoices

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: credit note and Debit note
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. 

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

Round‑off Calculation for Credit Note and Debit Note Documents

  • General behaviour

    • Rounding is a configuration option that applies to the total amount (including VAT/GST) of an invoicing document, whether it is an invoice, a credit memo (credit note) or a debit memo (debit note).
    • When the rounding function is active, the rounded total appears in its own line on the document, and the rounding difference is automatically distributed to the individual line items.
    • The same rounding key and rules are used for both debit‑type and credit‑type amounts; the configuration defines separate “Debit Rounding Unit” and “Credit Rounding Unit” values, but in the examples provided they are set to the same value.

  • Rounding rules by currency (as defined in the “Rounding Rules” view)

    Currency Debit Rounding Unit Credit Rounding Unit Amount Method (both) Example of rounding
    Swedish Krona (SEK)1.001.00“Round up or down to the nearest whole number.”138,22 SEK → 138,00 SEK; 138,68 SEK → 139,00 SEK; 138,50 SEK → 139,00 SEK
    Singapore Dollar (SGD)1.001.00“Round to the nearest 5 cent.”138.22 SGD → 138.20 SGD; 138.68 SGD → 138.70 SGD
    Swiss Franc (CHF)0.050.05“Round to the nearest whole number” (applied to 0.05‑CHF increments).582.02 CHF → 582.00 CHF; 582.03 CHF → 582.05 CHF

  • Key configuration steps (common to all currencies)

    1. Open Define Key for Invoice Rounding (ID 104810).
    2. In the Rounding Key view, confirm the C (Convergent FI‑CA) application area and enter the rounding key description.
    3. On the Rounding Key tab, specify transaction data for rounding carry‑forward and write‑off.
    4. In the Rounding Rules view, enter the relevant currency and set the debit/credit rounding units and methods as shown above.
    5. Save the entries.
    6. Assign the rounding key to the appropriate invoicing process via Assign Key for Invoice Rounding (ID 104811).

  • What the context does not specify

    • The documents do not provide a separate, detailed example for a debit note (debit memo) rounding calculation; they only illustrate rounding for invoices and credit memos.
    • Therefore, the exact rounding outcome for a debit note would follow the same unit and method defined for the “Debit Rounding Unit” in the relevant currency configuration.

The above information is derived directly from the provided configuration documentation for rounding in Convergent Invoicing.

In case this information doesn't help you resolve your issue, please let us know.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Adding custom field in "Receive Products" app
entry.content:
Dear @KhaingZinTun,

You're not limited to static fields, what matters is where the logic runs:

  • Frontend logic / custom actions (controller extensions): not available the app's synchronous views disable them in the adaptation project
  • Backend logic to populate field data: supported, via the app's released BAdIs

For your case, pulling Product Master Data into new fields should be handled through the Product Details Enhancement BAdI RETAIL_ST_PL_PRODUCT_ENH. This BAdI enriches the product data in the backend, and a UI field can bind to that data only if the data is exposed in the app’s data model/OData response

More details below:

  • SAP Help – Extensibility: Look Up Retail Products
  • SAP Help – Controller Extensions

Best Regards,

Dawid

During the initial scoping and deployment, we unfortunately missed including the scope items:

- 5KF

Based on the official “Availability Dependencies” documentation, these scope items cannot be added afterwards to an already deployed CBC workspace.

Since we are still at a very early stage (no productive configuration or dependent activities completed yet), we kindly request your support with one of the following options:

1. A full CBC Reset of our workspace  

   Affected systems / URLs:
   - Frontend (S/4HANA):  
        - CBC Backend:  
     OR

2. If a reset is not recommended or technically feasible, please advise on the best alternative approach, such as:
   - Re-provisioning of the CBC workspace / Customizing tenant (component:  XX‑S4C‑OPR‑SRV)
   - Creating a new workspace with correct initial scoping
   - Any SAP-recommended procedure to include the missing scope items

Our main objective is to ensure a clean and correct project setup before proceeding further in the Explore phase.

As we are still at a very early stage, data loss or reinitialization is fully acceptable.

We appreciate your guidance on the most efficient and recommended way forward.

Best regards,


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Reset of CBC configuration in customizing tenant nece...
entry.content:
Hi!

Thank you for your question.

As we understand, you identified that scope items 5HG and 5KF were not selected during the initial scoping activities in your SAP Central Business Configuration workspace.

The good news is that, if your workspace is still in the early implementation stages and the deployment has not yet been executed via Deploy Scope and Organizational Structure activity in CBC, there is no need for a tenant reset. In this situation, the recommended approach is to restart the setup process by deleting the workspace and perform the initial scoping again, ensuring that all required scope items are included from the beginning.

This is generally the most efficient path forward, as it allows you to establish the correct project scope before any downstream configuration activities, deployments, or dependencies are introduced and helps avoid potential complications later in the implementation.

As described in KBA 3377871 - Not possible to activate scope items 5HG and 5KF in SAP Central Business Configuration - SAP for Me, when deployment has not yet occurred, recreating the setup with the correct scope selection is the preferred approach. Once the required scope items have been included, you can proceed with the project activities as planned.

This is mentioned in "If the scope items 5HG and 5KF were not activated during the initial scoping phase and no content was deployed to the target system via activity Deploy Scope and Organizational Structure, the Workspace can be deleted and recreated from scratch. For more details, refer to SAP Help Portal SAP Help - Deleting a Workspace."

We hope this clarifies the available options and helps you move forward with confidence.

Best Regards!

entry.title: Re: Exclude non-working hours in Avg Cycle time in Signav...
entry.content:
Hi @manuelmeindl22 ,

Can you elaborate on this answer please? How would importing a factory calendar help with the cycle time calculation? 

For example, assume we have a holiday on January 1st, Event A occurring on December 31st, and Event B occurring on January 2nd. A standard cycle time calculation would yield 2 days. Incorporating our factory calendar, our desired cycle time is 1 day by excluding the January 1st holiday. In either case, the Signavio eventlog is the same:

 

c_caseidc_eventnamec_time
123Event ADecember 31st
123Event BJanuary 2nd

so how does incorporating a factory calendar help calculate the desired cycle time between the events?

An option would be to put all event collector code in one event collector to be able to create the entire eventlog in one script then do a lead/lag comparison of rows and then find the difference between subsequent events while excluding certain dates. However, this is not practical.

Thanks.

entry.title: Re: Project Cost Line Items – Loss of Cumulative and Commitment-Based Measures in Recent Releases
entry.content:
When raising this concern through official product support channels, the standard response (KBA 3742776 - APPS F6991 and F6992 do not display Commitments) states that the new applications are working as designed.

The recommended workaround provided by Product Support is to steer users back to the classical SAP GUI Report Writer/Painter transactions:

  • S_ALR_8701354 (Project Info System: Costs/Revenues/Expenditures/Funds)
  • S_ALR_87013542 (Actual/Commitment/Total/Plan in Project)

As SAP specialists striving to implement SAP Activate best practices and driving Clean Core governance, this workaround presents an architectural contradiction. We are pushing organizations to adopt the modern Fiori UX and deprecate legacy GUI reporting, yet the standard digital core forces a regression to 20-year-old ABAP report engines to perform core financial cross-module validations (PS-FI-CO-MM).

entry.title: Recurring deductions not editable in ONB2.0 hiring process
entry.content:
Hello community, 

Would like to invite everyone to vote for the following influence item that will benefit our Onboarding module users and end users:

https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/377027 

Sharing the brief here and please help us with your vote:

The current limitation prevents business teams from maintaining recurring allowances and deductions during the onboarding stage, which leads to incomplete compensation data at the time of hire and creates risks for downstream processes such as Payroll. It also introduces inefficiencies by requiring manual updates after employee creation and results in an inconsistent user experience, as this is the only Compensation portlet that does not function during onboarding. Enabling this capability earlier in the process would ensure complete and accurate data capture, reduce duplicate effort, improve data integrity, and streamline the overall hiring and payroll workflow for all stakeholders involved
 
#Onboarding2.0 ##ONB  #Onboarding #recruitment

entry.title: Re: Business Data Architecture: Defining Business Meaning
entry.content:
Hi Kristian,

 
I’ve seen similar behavior, especially from HANA calculation views.
 
From my experience, it really depends on the type of metric definition. In most cases, when I design metrics, I am mainly applying filters and relatively simple business rules on already harmonized data. In those scenarios, I haven’t really seen any major performance issues from defining the logic in views.
 
Where it can become different is if you have more complex calculations - for example metrics depending on multiple sources or layers of metrics built on top of each other. In those cases, I would typically persist the data on aggregated grain to avoid recalculating everything repeatedly. But it’s worth noting that this kind of complexity will usually impact performance regardless of whether it’s implemented in views or directly in analytical models.
 
That said, I still think it’s the better approach to define metrics in views rather than pushing them into analytical models. Even if there’s a small performance impact in some scenarios, the benefit is that you define the metric once and expose it through a reusable data product.
 
That makes it possible to reuse the same definition across multiple data products and analytical models without redefining it each time. In contrast, if the logic sits in analytical models, you quickly end up duplicating the same metric in multiple places.
 
So for me, it’s really about balance: default to defining metrics in a reusable data product, and only optimize or persist when the complexity actually justifies it.
 
Best regards
 
Allan Hald

entry.title: Re: Not able to find Cloud ALM service key
entry.content:
Hi, basim_wangde92,
It seems you may not have proper authorizations to access the Cloud ALM BTP Global/Subaccount to view this info within. -> check KBA 3123560 - Cannot access the SAP Cloud ALM Global Account or Sub account in SAP BTP 

There is a possibility, based on when your Cloud ALM provisioning date, that the service key may have been stored in Cloud ALM's Landscape Management.  -> Check KBA 3111953 - How to Retrieve the Service Key for SAP Cloud ALM - SAP for Me.

Help doc: Retrieving Service Credentials | SAP Help Portal and Managing Your Service Credentials | SAP Help Portal (share with your BTP Global Account administrator).

I hope this is helpful to you.

Best regards,
Paula
